papers.title: From Weed Evolution to Crop Design: A Computational Blueprint for a Novel, Synergistic Herbicide-Resistant Allele in Wheat.
papers.abstract: The escalating crisis of herbicide-resistant weeds threatens global wheat production. While key mutations are well-documented in weeds, the principles governing their interactions in wheat remain largely unknown. Here, we first developed a novel wheat germplasm carrying the acetolactate synthase (TaALS) Ser-627-Asn (S627N) mutation via ethyl methanesulfonate (EMS) mutagenesis. We then employed a computational design strategy to explore its synergy with the prevalent Trp-548-Leu (W548L) mutation-a combination not yet reported in nature. Integrated molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and free en
papers.summary: Researchers developed a computational strategy to design a novel herbicide-resistant wheat allele by combining two mutations (S627N and W548L) in the acetolactate synthase gene, using molecular dynamics simulations to predict their synergistic effects. The study demonstrates how insights from weed evolution can be translated into crop improvement, offering a blueprint for creating more durable herbicide resistance in wheat.

papers.title: Monodisperse PEG engineering for quantifiable surface conjugation on PLGA nanoparticles.
papers.abstract: Surface PEGylation is a well-established strategy to enhance the stability, functionality, and bioavailability of nanoparticles (NPs) in biomedical applications. However, the widespread use of polydisperse PEG derivatives limits the ability to precisely quantify surface PEGylation efficiency (mol%) and evaluate its effect on NP properties. In this study, we present a systematic approach to engineer PLGA NPs using structurally defined monodisperse PEG-diamine derivatives of varying chain lengths (PEG6, PEG26, PEG45), synthesized and purified by chromatographic methods and covalently attached vi
papers.summary: This paper presents a method to engineer PLGA nanoparticles with precisely defined monodisperse PEG chains, enabling accurate quantification of surface PEGylation efficiency. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional polydisperse PEG, allowing better control over nanoparticle properties for biomedical applications.

papers.title: Enterovirus RNase L inhibiting RNAs are highly conserved with limited phylogenetic distribution.
papers.abstract: RNA molecules form specific 3D structures that facilitate a variety of functions through interactions with other macromolecules. Many RNA viral genomes maintain these structures to interact with and evade host immunity machinery. One such element, the competitive inhibitor RNA (ciRNA), discovered in the protein coding region of the poliovirus serotype 1 (PV1) genome, inhibits a host antiviral protein, ribonuclease L (RNase L). Although some functionally essential structural motifs of the PV1 ciRNA have been studied, the extent of its evolutionary conservation and other structural requirements
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates the evolutionary conservation of a specific RNA structure (ciRNA) in enteroviruses that inhibits the host antiviral protein RNase L. The authors find that while ciRNA sequences are highly conserved among enteroviruses, they are present in only a limited subset of viral lineages, suggesting a specialized evolutionary role. This work provides insights into how viral RNA structures can evade host immunity and highlights the balance between conservation and phylogenetic restriction.

papers.title: Pep2Mol: 3D Molecule Generation Targeting Protein-Protein Interfaces with Diffusion Models.
papers.abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are central to biological processes. Designing small molecules that modulate dysregulated PPIs holds strong promise for targeting undruggable proteins. However, existing structure-based drug design approaches focus on well-defined small-molecule binding pockets and struggle to generalize to large, shallow, and chemically complex PPI interfaces. Here, we introduce Pep2Mol, a diffusion-based generative model for 3D molecule design that targets orthosteric PPI sites by explicitly incorporating binding peptides or proteins as structural guidance, moving beyond c
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Pep2Mol is a new AI model that uses diffusion to generate 3D small molecules capable of targeting protein-protein interaction (PPI) interfaces, which are typically difficult to drug due to their large and shallow surfaces. By incorporating peptide or protein structural guidance, the method overcomes limitations of traditional drug design approaches that focus on well-defined binding pockets. This work offers a promising computational strategy for designing modulators of previously 'undruggable' proteins.

papers.title: Mitochondrial Voltage-Dependent Anion Channel: From a Passive Pore to a Cellular Hub Through Protein Complexation.
papers.abstract: The voltage-dependent anion channel (VDAC) is the primary conduit for ion and metabolite transport across the mitochondrial outer membrane. Positioned at the interface between the cytosol and the mitochondrial compartment, VDAC is uniquely accessible to proteins on both sides of the membrane, making it an interaction hub whose biophysical properties and signaling functions are shaped by protein complexation in addition to its intrinsic pore specialization. Mammals express three isoforms-VDAC1, VDAC2, and VDAC3-sharing a conserved β-barrel scaffold with about 70% identity. However, minor differ
papers.summary: This review discusses how the voltage-dependent anion channel (VDAC) in mitochondria is not just a passive pore but a dynamic hub whose functions are regulated by interactions with other proteins. It highlights the three mammalian isoforms and how their protein complexation shapes biophysical properties and signaling.

papers.title: Integrated Screening Cascades for Ion-Channel Drug Discovery: Linking Structure, Electrophysiology, Safety Pharmacology, and Human-Relevant Models.
papers.abstract: Ion channels are validated drug targets, but they remain difficult to study as their pharmacology is influenced by rapid gating, conformational state transitions, cell-type-specific expression, and narrow safety margins. Recent advances in cryo-electron microscopy, structure-based in silico screening, machine-learning-guided prioritization, optical high-throughput screening, automated patch-clamp electrophysiology, and human-relevant organoid or microphysiological system (MPS) models are transforming this field. In this expanded review, we examine how these modalities can be integrated into a
papers.summary: This review discusses how integrating cryo-EM, machine learning, automated patch-clamp, and human organoid models can overcome challenges in ion-channel drug discovery, such as rapid gating and safety margins. It highlights a shift toward multi-modal screening cascades that link structure, function, and human-relevant biology to improve hit identification and safety assessment.

papers.title: In Silico Design of Pyrimidine Derivatives as Potential α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: QSAR, Molecular Docking, ADMET, and Molecular Dynamics Studies.
papers.abstract: Diabetes mellitus remains a major metabolic disorder requiring the development of new and effective α-glucosidase inhibitors. The present study aimed to identify, design, and optimize novel 3-amino-2,4-diarylbenzo[4,5]imidazo[1,2-α]pyrimidine derivatives with promising inhibitory activity against the α-glucosidase enzyme using a comprehensive in silico strategy. Approximately 300 molecular descriptors were calculated to characterize a dataset of 32 compounds (Peytam et al.) and to investigate the structural factors governing their biological activity. Based on these descriptors, a multiple lin
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to design new pyrimidine-based compounds that could inhibit α-glucosidase, a target for diabetes treatment. By analyzing molecular features and simulating how the compounds interact with the enzyme, the researchers identified promising drug candidates with favorable properties.

papers.title: Structure-Guided Discovery and Biochemical Validation of Novel Small-Molecule Inhibitors Predicted to Target the CCHFV OTU Protease Y89-W99 Pocket.
papers.abstract: Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) remains a major public health threat due to its high mortality rates and the absence of approved antiviral therapies. The viral ovarian tumor (OTU) protease is a critical virulence factor that suppresses host innate immunity through its deubiquitinase activity, making it an attractive therapeutic target. In this study, we employed a structure-guided approach to identify and validate novel small-molecule inhibitors targeting the non-catalytic Y89-W99 pocket of the OTU protease. Recombinant OTU protease was successfully expressed, purified, and refol
papers.summary: Researchers used a structure-guided approach to discover small-molecule inhibitors targeting a specific pocket on the Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus OTU protease, a key virulence factor. These inhibitors could potentially lead to new antiviral therapies for a disease with high mortality and no approved treatments.

papers.title: Cell-Extracellular Matrix Feedback Results in Spontaneous Cell Polarization and Heterogeneous Remodeling in 3D Isotropic and Aligned Discrete-Fiber Models of Cell-Mediated Remodeling.
papers.abstract: The extracellular matrix (ECM) is a dynamic fiber environment containing structural information that significantly impacts cell behavior. Recent experimental work has demonstrated that cells also have significant capacity to remodel their microenvironment, often resulting in ECM heterogeneity. We present an open-source molecular dynamics platform that simulates cell-mediated remodeling wherein cells plasticly remodel their microenvironment and respond to induced structural heterogeneity over multiple retraction cycles.
papers.summary: This paper presents an open-source molecular dynamics platform that simulates how cells remodel their surrounding extracellular matrix (ECM) and respond to the resulting structural changes over multiple cycles. The model captures spontaneous cell polarization and heterogeneous ECM remodeling in both isotropic and aligned fiber environments, providing insights into cell-ECM feedback mechanisms.

papers.title: Improving the yield and biological activity of sage essential oil using NADES: in vitro and in silico insights.
papers.abstract: This study investigates the efficacy of Natural Deep Eutectic Solvents (NADESs) as innovative green pretreatments for the hydrodistillation of sage (Salvia officinalis L.) leaves, focusing on essential oil (EO) yield, phytochemical modulation, and biological activity. Three choline chloride (ChCl)-based NADESs were synthesized and characterized via ATR-FTIR. The results revealed that NADES pretreatments significantly enhanced extraction efficiency, with the ChCl: Malic acid pretreatment achieving the highest EO yield of 2.53% (v/w) compared to 2.31% for the water-based control. GC-MS analysis
papers.summary: This study shows that using natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) as a pretreatment for extracting sage essential oil can increase yield and improve biological activity compared to traditional water-based methods. The choline chloride-malic acid NADES gave the highest oil yield (2.53% vs 2.31% control) and altered the oil's chemical composition. The findings suggest NADES are a promising green alternative for essential oil extraction with potential for enhanced bioactivity.

papers.title: Integration of molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, and free energy calculations identifies a novel flavonoid glycoside as a PCSK9 inhibitor for hypercholesterolemia.
papers.abstract: Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) significantly influences plasma low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) homeostasis by promoting the degradation of LDL receptors. While monoclonal antibody therapies are effective, their widespread application is limited by high costs and invasive administration, necessitating the development of oral small-molecule inhibitors for hypercholesterolemia management. This study evaluates the therapeutic potential of a specific flavonoid glycoside, 7-(alpha-D-Glucopyranosyloxy)-5-hydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4 H-1-benzopyran-4-one, as a novel
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify a flavonoid glycoside compound that may inhibit PCSK9, a protein that regulates cholesterol levels by breaking down LDL receptors. The compound shows potential as an oral small-molecule alternative to expensive antibody therapies for managing high cholesterol.

papers.title: Systems-level design of a multi-epitope immunotherapeutic vaccine targeting EBV-associated oncogenesis.
papers.abstract: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is an oncogenic herpesvirus associated with multiple lymphoid and epithelial malignancies. Despite extensive investigation of EBV vaccine strategies, effective therapeutic approaches capable of targeting established EBV-associated cancers remain limited. In this study, we developed an integrated immunoinformatics and structure-guided framework for the design and prioritization of therapeutic multi-epitope vaccine candidates targeting both structural glycoproteins (gp350, gB, gH/gL, and gp42) and latency-associated proteins (EBNA1, LMP1, LMP2, and BZLF1). Sixteen multi-
papers.summary: This study uses computational immunoinformatics and structural biology to design a multi-epitope vaccine targeting Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) proteins involved in both infection and cancer. The vaccine candidate aims to stimulate immune responses against EBV-associated malignancies, addressing a gap in therapeutic options.

papers.title: Designing novel adenine clamp-CD73 mutants delineating surface expression and enzyme activity through in silico and in vitro engineering.
papers.abstract: NT5E, a 5' Ectonucleotidase, is an emerging hotspot for cancer immunotherapies. However, available enzymatic inhibitors have shown limited promise, indicating activity-independent roles. Delineating activity-dependent and independent roles of this protein with precision requisites identification of critical residues impacting activity without hampering surface expression/stability. Conserved Phenylalanine (F) at 417 and 500 positions, which form a π stack with substrate-AMP, was chosen as the region of interest for in silico mutagenesis. F417 and F500 were mutated to Valine (V) individually us
papers.summary: The study identifies two conserved phenylalanine residues (F417 and F500) in the CD73 protein that are critical for its enzymatic activity but not for surface expression, enabling the design of mutants that separate activity-dependent and independent functions. Using in silico mutagenesis and in vitro validation, the authors show that mutating these residues to valine reduces enzyme activity without affecting protein stability or surface localization. This provides a tool to dissect the role of CD73 in cancer immunotherapy beyond its enzymatic function.

papers.title: Dynamics-enhanced molecular property prediction guided by deep learning.
papers.abstract: Molecular property prediction (MPP) is a key challenge in computational biology and drug discovery. Traditional approaches mostly rely on feature representations yielded from static structures of molecules, ignoring their dynamic nature. The scarcity of dynamics data in public databases and the complexity of learning such high-dimensional data made it more difficult for dynamics-involved studies. Accordingly, we built a series of dynamics datasets for MPP tasks by performing comprehensive molecular dynamics (MD) simulations on different molecules. In addition, we proposed a dynamically enhance
papers.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of molecular property prediction by incorporating molecular dynamics data, which captures the dynamic behavior of molecules rather than relying solely on static structures. The authors created new dynamics datasets through molecular dynamics simulations and developed a deep learning method that leverages this dynamic information to improve prediction accuracy.

papers.title: Disruption of quorum sensing and biofilm formation by lawsone in gram-negative bacteria.
papers.abstract: Antimicrobial resistance is a major global health threat, exacerbated by bacterial virulence mechanisms such as quorum sensing (QS) and biofilm formation, which enhance pathogenicity and reduce antibiotic susceptibility. Targeting QS offers a promising antivirulence strategy that may attenuate pathogenic traits without imposing selective pressure. In this study, lawsone was evaluated for anti-QS and antibiofilm activity against Chromobacterium violaceum, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Serratia marcescens. Lawsone significantly inhibited QS-regulated virulence factors, with reductions in violacein
papers.summary: This study shows that lawsone, a natural compound, can disrupt quorum sensing and biofilm formation in several gram-negative bacteria, potentially offering a new antivirulence strategy to combat antimicrobial resistance.

papers.title: Single-sequence based gFET-aptasensors for the discrimination of apo- and holo-RBP4 in human serum.
papers.abstract: Retinol-binding protein 4 (RBP4) is a key transporter of all-trans-retinol (vitamin A), circulating in blood as either holo-RBP4 (retinol-bound) or apo-RBP4 (retinol-free). Dysregulated RBP4 levels, particularly an imbalance between apo- and holo-RBP4, have been implicated in a range of metabolic and cardiovascular diseases. Current detection methods such as ELISA and Western blot lack the specificity to distinguish these two forms. Here, we present an aptamer-based biosensing strategy that overcomes this limitation by using selected single aptamer sequences for the precise and selective quant
papers.summary: This paper describes a new aptamer-based biosensor (gFET-aptasensor) that can distinguish between the retinol-bound (holo) and retinol-free (apo) forms of RBP4 in human serum, which current methods like ELISA cannot do. The sensor uses single aptamer sequences to achieve precise and selective quantification of each form, potentially aiding in the diagnosis of metabolic and cardiovascular diseases linked to RBP4 imbalance.

papers.title: Mechanistic insight into signal bias by the agonist-dependent conformational dynamics of GPR84.
papers.abstract: GPR84 is an orphan class A GPCR primarily expressed in immune cells, where it plays key roles in inflammation and metabolism. Here, we present the cryo-electron microscopy structures of the GPR84-Gi complex bound to the G protein-biased agonist DL-175, and the inactive state of GPR84 bound to the antagonist GLPG1205. Combined with signaling assays and molecular dynamics simulations, these structures elucidate the conformational landscape spanning the inactive and G protein-biased active states of GPR84, providing a mechanistic basis for biased agonism. Notably, steric interactions between DL-1
papers.summary: This study reveals how the GPR84 receptor, found on immune cells, adopts different shapes when bound to a biased agonist versus an antagonist, explaining why some drugs can selectively activate certain signaling pathways. Using cryo-electron microscopy and computer simulations, the authors show that the agonist DL-175 stabilizes a specific active conformation that preferentially engages G proteins over other pathways. These findings provide a structural blueprint for designing more selective drugs targeting GPR84 in inflammatory diseases.

papers.title: RP-HPLC quantification and separation of isomeric bacosides and exploration of their anti-schizophrenic potential by network pharmacology.
papers.abstract: Schizophrenia is a chronic and heterogeneous neuropsychiatric disorder involving positive, negative, and cognitive symptoms. Antipsychotic drugs prescribed currently are effective in controlling positive symptoms but offer limited benefits for negative and cognitive impairments as they focus on single-target or narrow-mechanism treatment strategies without sufficiently addressing the multi-factorial dysfunction underlying schizophrenia. Therefore, multi-target natural compounds like Bacopa monnieri (Brahmi) are increasingly being explored as potential therapeutic candidates for neurological di
papers.summary: This study develops an RP-HPLC method to quantify and separate isomeric bacosides from Bacopa monnieri, and uses network pharmacology to explore their potential anti-schizophrenic effects. The findings suggest these natural compounds may act on multiple targets relevant to schizophrenia, offering a multi-target therapeutic approach beyond current single-mechanism drugs.

papers.title: Novel seleno-ureido hybrids targeting head and neck cancer and beyond: design, synthesis, and apoptosis-mediated anticancer evaluation.
papers.abstract: This study systematically designed a series of urea-linked organoselenium hybrids via molecular hybridization, combining the redox-active selenium pharmacophore with the hydrogen-bond-forming urea motif. The synthesized compounds featured both mono- and bis-ureido frameworks with various selenium substituents (methyl, allyl, and benzyl) and differing linker topologies. A biological evaluation of a panel of eight human tumor cells exhibited promising antiproliferative activity, with average growth inhibition percentages (% GI) ranging from low to high potency. Compound HB204 possessed the great
papers.summary: This study designed and synthesized a new class of selenium-urea hybrid compounds that show promising anticancer activity against head and neck cancer and other tumor cell lines. The most potent compound, HB204, demonstrated strong antiproliferative effects by inducing apoptosis in cancer cells.

papers.title: P2Y12-oriented computational screening and functional validation identify antiplatelet compounds with antithrombotic activity.
papers.abstract: Arterial thrombosis is driven by excessive platelet activation, in which the P2Y12 receptor is a key amplifier of ADP-dependent signaling and a clinically validated antiplatelet target. P2Y12-oriented computational screening may facilitate the discovery of structurally diverse compounds with antiplatelet activity and provide new starting points for antithrombotic intervention.
papers.summary: This study used computational screening targeting the P2Y12 receptor to identify new compounds that inhibit platelet activation, followed by functional validation showing antithrombotic activity. The approach successfully discovered structurally diverse antiplatelet agents, offering potential new leads for treating arterial thrombosis.

papers.title: Novel acidic ionic liquid catalyst based on C8-modified caffeine for high-performance sustainable synthesis of pyridodipyrimidines and a study on their carbonic anhydrase inhibitory activity.
papers.abstract: In this study, we designed and synthesized a novel acidic ionic liquid based on a C8-modified caffeine scaffold via ion pair formation between the 8-piperazinyl-caffeine derivative and trichloroacetic acid, yielding [8-PC]TCA. The prepared acidic ionic liquid was then used as an efficient catalyst in multicomponent reactions (MCRs) to synthesize pyrido[2,3-d:5,6-d']dipyrimidine derivatives in H2O/EtOH mixture at 80 °C. The catalyst structure was confirmed by FT-IR, 1H NMR spectroscopy and melting point analysis. The catalytic performance was confirmed by high yield (87-98%) of products obtaine
papers.summary: This study reports the design of a new acidic ionic liquid catalyst derived from caffeine, which efficiently promotes the synthesis of pyridodipyrimidine compounds in high yields. The synthesized compounds were also evaluated for their ability to inhibit carbonic anhydrase, an enzyme relevant to drug discovery.

papers.title: In silico design and validation of high-affinity RNA aptamers for SARS-CoV-2 comparable to neutralizing antibodies.
papers.abstract: Nucleic acid aptamers hold promise for clinical applications, yet understanding their molecular binding mechanisms to target proteins, and efficiently optimizing their binding affinities, remain challenging. Here, we present CAAMO (Computer-Aided Aptamer Modeling and Optimization), which integrates in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to accelerate the development of aptamer-based RNA therapeutics. Starting from the sequence information of a reported RNA aptamer, Ta, for the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, our CAAMO method first determines its binding mode with the spike protein's r
papers.summary: This paper presents CAAMO, a computational method that combines in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to develop high-affinity RNA aptamers targeting the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. The approach successfully generated aptamers with binding affinities comparable to neutralizing antibodies, demonstrating a powerful tool for accelerating RNA therapeutic development.

papers.title: Structural insights into multitargeting Mycobacterium tuberculosis Pkn kinases.
papers.abstract: Tuberculosis remains one of the leading major global health challenges, driven by the emergence of multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant bacterial strains. Resistant strains complicate treatment, which often requires prolonged use of toxic second- and third-line drugs. Protein phosphorylation plays critical roles in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, with serine/threonine kinases PknA, PknB, and PknG being essential for survival, virulence, and persistence. In this study, we screened an in-house kinase inhibitor library to identify compounds targeting these kinases. Four structurally div
papers.summary: This study identifies four compounds that inhibit three essential protein kinases (PknA, PknB, PknG) in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, offering a potential multitargeting strategy against drug-resistant tuberculosis. Structural insights into how these inhibitors bind to the kinases could guide development of more effective antituberculosis drugs.

papers.title: Design and Synthesis of Novel Imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine Derivatives, Evaluation of Their Activity Against Hepatitis C Virus and In Silico Prediction of Their Binding Mode to NS4B Protein.
papers.abstract: A series of suitably substituted imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine derivatives was synthesized, starting from 4-amino-2-chloropyridine, and their antiviral activity against hepatitis C virus (HCV) genotype 1b (GT1b) was evaluated in Huh5.2 replicon cells. Some of the N3-substituted analogs exhibited potent anti-HCV activity (EC50 = 6.61-47.24 μM), as determined by assessing their individual effects on viral RNA replication and protein expression levels. Among these, the most promising compound 30b demonstrated synergistic antiviral activity when combined with clinically approved direct-acting anti-HCV th
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized a new class of imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine compounds and tested them against hepatitis C virus. Several compounds showed moderate antiviral activity, with the best one working synergistically with an existing HCV drug. The study also used computer modeling to predict how these compounds might bind to the viral NS4B protein.

papers.title: Phytochemical profiling, network pharmacology, and molecular dynamics simulations of Sinularia levi metabolites as potential α-glucosidase inhibitors in metabolic syndrome.
papers.abstract: This study has investigated the phytochemical composition and therapeutic potential of the Red Sea soft coral Sinularia levi. Five compounds (1-5) comprising three glyceryl derivatives, a ceramide, and a diterpene were isolated and characterized. To elucidate their role in metabolic syndrome, an integrated network pharmacology and structure-based modeling workflow was employed. This approach identified 45 shared targets between S. levi metabolites and disease proteins, clustered into inflammatory and metabolic nodes, with α-glucosidase (GAA) emerging as a primary target. Molecular docking agai
papers.summary: This study isolated five compounds from the Red Sea soft coral Sinularia levi and used network pharmacology and molecular docking to identify α-glucosidase as a primary target for treating metabolic syndrome. The findings suggest these marine-derived metabolites may serve as potential α-glucosidase inhibitors, offering a natural product-based approach for managing metabolic disorders.

papers.title: Cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin.
papers.abstract: Fibronectin (FN) and transglutaminase 2 (TG2) engage in a high-affinity interaction. This interaction affects anchoring, migration and survival of cells, and high cellular expression of TG2 is associated with metastasis potential of cancers. Previous work has mapped the interaction to involve the N-terminal part of TG2 and an N-terminally located 45 kDa domain of FN (FN45). In particular, the I8 module of FN45 is reported to be necessary and sufficient for TG2 binding. Here, we present the cryo-EM structure of a complex between human TG2 and FN45 at 2.8 Å resolution. In the structure, the dist
papers.summary: This study presents a high-resolution cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 (TG2) and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin (FN45), revealing the atomic details of their interaction. The structure shows how the I8 module of FN45 binds to the N-terminal part of TG2, providing insights into how this interaction influences cell adhesion, migration, and cancer metastasis. This structural information could aid in developing therapies targeting TG2-fibronectin interactions in metastatic cancers.

papers.title: Rational engineering of the Arabidopsis thaliana plant cysteine oxidase 4 active site can reduce biochemical activity and improve submergence tolerance.
papers.abstract: The increase in flooding events due to climate change is having devastating effects on food security. Plants can respond to submergence by reducing their energy requirements and switching their metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to fermentation. This response is promoted by group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs) whose stability is dependent on oxygen availability; in normoxia, plant cysteine oxidase (PCO) enzymes catalyze oxidation of ERF-VII N-terminal Cys residues to trigger ERF-VII degradation via the Cys/Arg-N-degron pathway. In hypoxia, reduced PCO activity prevents ERF-VII
papers.summary: This study shows that rationally engineering the active site of the Arabidopsis thaliana plant cysteine oxidase 4 (PCO4) can reduce its biochemical activity, which in turn improves submergence tolerance in plants. By slowing the oxygen-dependent degradation of group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs), the modified enzyme helps plants maintain stress responses during flooding. This work offers a potential strategy for engineering flood-tolerant crops to address climate change impacts on food security.

papers.title: Targeting GALNT7 Disrupts the TAZ O-GalNAcylation Feedback Loop to Suppress Gallbladder Cancer Progression.
papers.abstract: Gallbladder cancer (GBC) is a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and dismal prognosis. Identifying the factors driving GBC progression is crucial for developing potent preventive and therapeutic approaches. Here, using quantitative proteomics, we identify GALNT7 as the most significantly upregulated glycosyltransferase in GBC tissues, associated with adverse clinical outcomes. Mechanistically, GALNT7 physically interacts with TAZ and catalyzes O-GalNAcylation at Ser307, thereby inhibiting K48-linked ubiquitination through the recruitment of the deubiquitinase USP7 and stabilizi
papers.summary: This study identifies the glycosyltransferase GALNT7 as a key driver of gallbladder cancer progression. GALNT7 stabilizes the TAZ protein by adding O-GalNAc sugars, which prevents its degradation and activates a cancer-promoting feedback loop. Targeting GALNT7 disrupts this loop and suppresses tumor growth, offering a potential new therapeutic strategy.

papers.title: Molecular Dynamics Simulation of Nanostructured Grazynes for Water Desalination.
papers.abstract: The search for selective desalination membranes is a topic of great importance for producing freshwater. Here, the potential use of grazynes  2D C-based porous membranes that combine graphene stripes linked by acetylenic linkages  as desalination membranes has been explored using molecular dynamics simulations. Five different grazynes with pore sizes between 1 and 21 Å2 were considered under pressures of 150 to 210 MPa. Two of these grazynes proved to be completely impermeable, while the other three  [1],[2]{2}, [1],[2]{3}, and [1],[3]{2}  demonstrated a clear water flow that increased alm
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to evaluate grazynes—2D carbon-based porous membranes—for water desalination. Among five grazynes with varying pore sizes, three showed clear water flow under pressure, suggesting potential as selective desalination membranes.

papers.title: Development of Radioligand Therapy-Responsive Syngeneic Prostate Models through Murine Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Point Mutation.
papers.abstract: Robust, reproducible, and meaningful research requires suitable murine preclinical models that effectively mimic the disease and possess a functional immune system. In the context of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) studies and with all the opportunities offered by theranostics, particularly prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA)-targeted therapies, the development of PSMA-positive murine models is essential for evaluating and further exploring these therapies in immunocompetent animals. However, strategies based on the transfection of murine cell lines with the human
papers.summary: This study addresses the lack of suitable mouse models for testing PSMA-targeted radioligand therapies by engineering murine prostate cancer cell lines to express a modified version of PSMA. The authors created syngeneic, immunocompetent models that better mimic human disease, enabling more accurate preclinical evaluation of these therapies.

papers.title: Mechano-reprogramming of tumor-associated macrophages by engineering nanogel stiffness for suppression of tumor development.
papers.abstract: Nanoparticle mechanical properties, as critical factors in tumor targeting drug delivery, have recently been revealed to regulate cellular biological functions. While macroscopic mechanical stimuli are demonstrated to reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), it is unclear how TAMs respond to microscopic stimuli from nanoparticles. Herein, we demonstrated that the stiff 15%NGs presented stronger interactions with integrin than the soft 2%NGs, promoting the polymerization of actin and the activation of yes-associated protein 1. Therefore, the pro-inflammatory phenotype of TAMs was enhanced
papers.summary: This study shows that the stiffness of nanogels can reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) from a pro-tumor to a pro-inflammatory state. Stiffer nanogels interact more strongly with integrin on TAMs, activating actin polymerization and YAP1 signaling to enhance anti-tumor immune responses. This mechano-reprogramming strategy offers a new approach for cancer immunotherapy by engineering nanoparticle mechanical properties.

papers.title: Subtype-selective binding of milk β-casomorphins across the opioid receptor family revealed by molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation.
papers.abstract: β-casomorphins (BCMs) are opioid-like peptides produced during the digestion of β-casein in the gastrointestinal tract. They are also involved in other gastrointestinal and neurophysiological processes. Although the effect of BCM7 on the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) has been extensively studied, the molecular interaction mechanisms of other BCMs with different opioid receptors have not been elucidated. This study aimed to investigate the receptor-specific binding behavior of BCM3-BCM11 across the μ-, δ-, κ-, and nociceptin/orphanin FQ peptide (NOP) opioid receptors using structure-based computation
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to investigate how different milk-derived opioid peptides (β-casomorphins) bind to various opioid receptors. The findings reveal subtype-selective binding patterns across μ-, δ-, κ-, and NOP receptors, extending beyond the well-studied BCM7-μ receptor interaction.

papers.title: Design and Engineering of an Artificial Bifunctional N-Deacetylase/N-Sulfotransferase for the Biosynthesis of N-Sulfated Heparosan.
papers.abstract: Heparin is a widely used anticoagulant, while its traditional animal-derived production faces significant challenges in safety and scalability. The active expression of the initiating-step bifunctional enzyme N-deacetylase/N-sulfotransferase (NDST) in bacteria remains a key bottleneck in heparin biosynthesis. Here, we firstly mined and characterized diverse polysaccharide N-deacetylases (NDases) in Escherichia coli and revealed several NDases that are active toward the deacetylation of heparosan. An artificial NDDr-RL-ST was designed and constructed by fusing an active NDase with a heterogenou
papers.summary: Researchers engineered an artificial bifunctional enzyme (NDDr-RL-ST) by fusing a bacterial N-deacetylase with a heterologous sulfotransferase, enabling efficient biosynthesis of N-sulfated heparosan in E. coli. This overcomes a key bottleneck in heparin production, offering a scalable and safer alternative to animal-derived heparin.

papers.title: Aromatic π-stacking stabilizes an α-helical SARS-CoV-2 MPER peptide that mimics the post-fusion spike and enables potent antiviral activity.
papers.abstract: Membrane fusion between SARS-CoV-2 and host cells is mediated by the spike protein and involves the membrane-proximal external region (MPER), a tryptophan-rich sequence implicated in viral entry. Here, we investigated the feasibility of MPER-derived peptides as potential antiviral agents and examined the structural determinants underlying their activity. A series of truncated MPER peptides was synthesized and evaluated for antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 in cellular assays, revealing potent inhibitory potency against different variants, with nanomolar IC50 values associated with sequence
papers.summary: This study shows that short peptides derived from the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein's membrane-proximal external region (MPER) can potently inhibit viral entry, with aromatic π-stacking interactions stabilizing an α-helical structure that mimics the post-fusion spike. The optimized peptides achieved nanomolar antiviral activity against multiple SARS-CoV-2 variants, suggesting a new avenue for peptide-based COVID-19 therapeutics.

papers.title: Multiscale Investigation of the Factors Governing Ice-Asphalt Interfacial Adhesion Strength: Insights from Pull-Off Tests and Molecular Simulations.
papers.abstract: Under low-temperature and high-humidity conditions, stable ice layers readily form on asphalt pavements in cold regions, and the enhanced ice-asphalt interfacial adhesion significantly increases deicing difficulty and traffic safety risks. To clarify the factors governing ice-asphalt interfacial adhesion strength, this study combines macroscopic pull-off tests and molecular dynamics simulations to systematically investigate the effects of interfacial contact area, temperature, pull-off rate, and molecular characteristics of representative asphalt components. The pull-off results show that adhe
papers.summary: This study investigates ice-asphalt interfacial adhesion strength under cold, humid conditions using both physical pull-off tests and molecular dynamics simulations. The findings clarify how factors like contact area, temperature, and asphalt composition affect deicing difficulty and traffic safety.

papers.title: Selection of Novel DNA Aptamers against HPV16 Biomarker, E6: An In Silico Approach.
papers.abstract: HPV16 E6 oncoprotein is a critical driver of cervical cancer, necessitating high-specificity diagnostic and therapeutic agents. This study presents an in silico selection of DNA aptamers targeting the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein through a pipeline of structure-based virtual screening and 250 ns Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, performed with GROMACS 2019 and the CHARMM36M. Using AutoDock VinaXB and HADDOCK, eight candidates were evaluated against the F2 RNA benchmark. MM/PBSA calculations identified Apt 9 (-143.76 kcal/mol) as the most potent lead, surpassing the affinity of the F2 control (-142.
papers.summary: This study used computer simulations to identify DNA aptamers that bind to the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein, a key driver of cervical cancer. Through virtual screening and molecular dynamics simulations, the researchers identified Apt 9 as a promising candidate with binding affinity superior to a known benchmark. This in silico approach offers a rapid and cost-effective method for developing diagnostic and therapeutic agents against HPV-related cancers.

papers.title: Molecular Dynamics Simulation of the Interfacial Characteristics of Functionalized Carbon Nanotube-Polyimide Composites.
papers.abstract: Insufficient interfacial interaction between nanoconductive materials and polymer matrices severely limits the mechanical, electrical, and pressure-sensing properties. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), widely used as polymer reinforcements due to their excellent properties, can significantly enhance the mechanical performance of nanocomposites by improving the interfacial interactions with the matrix. Given the diversity of functionalized CNTs, a systematic study of their interfacial bonding mechanisms is of great importance for both scientific research and engineering applications. To this end, this s
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to investigate how functionalized carbon nanotubes improve interfacial bonding with polyimide matrices, aiming to enhance the mechanical and electrical properties of nanocomposites. The work systematically examines different functionalization strategies to identify optimal interfacial interactions, which could inform the design of better polymer composite materials.

papers.title: Pharmacophore-based virtual screening, molecular docking, MD simulation, MM-GBSA energy calculation and DFT analysis for the in silico discovery of a SaFtsZ inhibitor.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of antimicrobial resistance demands the discovery of new antibacterial targets and inhibitors. Staphylococcus aureus filamenting temperature-sensitive protein (SaFtsZ), an essential cytoskeletal protein involved in bacterial cytokinesis and Z-ring formation, has gained attention as a promising target for antibacterial drug discovery. In the present study, an integrated computational strategy involving pharmacophore mapping, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and density functional theory (DFT) analysis was employed to identify potential SaFtsZ inhibitor
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to find potential inhibitors of SaFtsZ, a bacterial protein essential for cell division in Staphylococcus aureus. By combining pharmacophore modeling, molecular docking, and simulations, the authors identified promising drug candidates that could help combat antimicrobial resistance.

papers.title: Recent Progress in the Development of Selective MAGL Modulators (2020-2026).
papers.abstract: Monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) is a key enzyme at the interface between the endocannabinoid system and lipid metabolism, playing a pivotal role in the hydrolysis of the endocannabinoid 2-arachidonoylglycerol and in the regulation of lipid mediators involved in inflammation, pain, neurodegeneration and cancer. Owing to its therapeutic relevance, MAGL has emerged as an attractive pharmacological target, stimulating extensive research efforts aimed at the development of potent and selective modulators of its activity. Advances in medicinal chemistry, together with the increasing application of in
papers.summary: This review summarizes recent progress (2020-2026) in developing selective modulators of monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL), a key enzyme linking the endocannabinoid system to lipid metabolism and implicated in inflammation, pain, neurodegeneration, and cancer. It highlights advances in medicinal chemistry and the growing use of in silico methods to design potent and selective MAGL inhibitors.

papers.title: Screening of core targets for Di(2-ethylhexyl) Phthalate-related gastric cancer based on machine learning, molecular docking, and SHAP analysis.
papers.abstract: Given the existing uncertainties regarding the link between Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) exposure and gastric cancer (GC) progression, this study aimed to clarify their association, identify the toxic targets of DEHP, and elucidate the underlying molecular mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning, molecular docking, and SHAP analysis to identify core molecular targets linking the plasticizer DEHP to gastric cancer progression. The findings help clarify the potential toxic mechanisms of DEHP in gastric cancer, though the work is computational and requires experimental validation.

papers.title: Exploring the Distinct Regulatory Actions and Molecular Pathways of Coptisine Through Network Pharmacology: Insights Into Its Influence on Platelet Activation and Thrombus Development.
papers.abstract: To investigate the potential targets and underlying mechanisms of coptisine in regulating platelet activation and thrombosis by integrating an approach combining network pharmacology and experimental validation. An in vivo carotid artery thrombosis model induced by FeCl3-mediated endothelial injury was established in mice to evaluate the antithrombotic effects of coptisine. A combination of network pharmacology prediction and both in vivo and in vitro experimental validation was employed to elucidate the antithrombotic mechanisms of coptisine. Molecular docking simulations were performed using
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and experimental validation to show that coptisine, a natural compound, inhibits platelet activation and thrombus formation in a mouse model. The research identifies potential molecular targets and pathways, suggesting coptisine may be a candidate for antithrombotic therapy.

papers.title: Eu(III), Tb(III), and Gd(III) Complexes With Dual Ligand Architecture: Synthesis, Characterization, Photoluminescence, Cytotoxic, and Molecular Docking Studies.
papers.abstract: By using the naphthalene group-containing β-diketone ligand and 5-nitro-1,10-phenanthroline, three novel heteroleptic eight-coordinate complexes of Eu(III), Tb(III), and Gd(III) have been synthesized. Methods such as elemental analysis, MALDI TOF-MS, UV-Vis, and FT-IR were used to determine the structural characterization of these compounds. The photoluminescent characteristics of the synthesized complexes of Eu(III), Tb(III), and Gd(III) were further investigated. The ligand (L1) and co-ligand 5-nitro-1,10-phenanthroline (L2) and its three lanthanide complexes [Eu(L1)3(L2)]·3H2O, [Tb(L1)3(L2)
papers.summary: This study reports the synthesis and characterization of three novel lanthanide complexes (Eu, Tb, Gd) using a naphthalene-based β-diketone ligand and a phenanthroline co-ligand. The complexes were analyzed for their photoluminescent properties and evaluated for cytotoxic activity against cancer cell lines, with molecular docking studies suggesting potential biological targets. The work provides new luminescent lanthanide compounds with possible applications in bioimaging and anticancer therapy.

papers.title: Enhancing the Antimicrobial Potency of Self-Assembling Antibiotics by Co-Assembly-Based Aggregation Modulation.
papers.abstract: The activity of many antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), a promising alternative to classical antibiotics, depends strongly on their aggregation-propensity and weak peptide association favor antimicrobial function, whereas stable assembly can reduce activity. Thus, modulation of intermolecular interactions offers a feasible way to enhance the potency of existing AMPs. However, a general strategy to achieve this objective has not been established. Here, we demonstrate that the spectrum of activity of Gram-positive-specific self-assembling AMP Fmoc-phenylalanine can be broadened by co-assembly with n
papers.summary: This study presents a co-assembly strategy to modulate the aggregation of self-assembling antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), broadening the activity spectrum of a Gram-positive-specific AMP to include Gram-negative bacteria. By co-assembling Fmoc-phenylalanine with other molecules, the authors demonstrate a generalizable approach to enhance the potency of existing AMPs through controlled intermolecular interactions.

papers.title: FlowDock: A unified flow-based framework for flexible protein-ligand docking and binding affinity prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand complexes and binding affinity is critical for hit identification and optimization for structure-based drug design. Traditional docking simulates binding processes with searching algorithms guided by energy-scoring functions, which are quite computationally expensive and time-intensive. In contrast, deep learning approaches offer a cost-effective alternative, yet often generate conformations with limited physicochemical validity and fail to account for protein flexibility. To address these pitfalls, we propose FlowDock, a multitask framework enhanced by Ba
papers.summary: FlowDock is a new deep learning framework that simultaneously predicts protein-ligand complex structures and binding affinity, overcoming limitations of traditional docking (slow) and prior AI methods (poor physical validity, no protein flexibility). It uses a flow-based approach to efficiently generate realistic binding poses and affinity scores, offering a faster and more accurate tool for drug discovery.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence in biologic drug discovery: A review of methodological evolution and therapeutic applications.
papers.abstract: Biologic drugs, primarily comprising proteins and nucleic acids, have emerged as powerful therapeutic modalities; however, their discovery and optimization are often hindered by their inherent complexity. The advent of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly deep learning, is catalyzing a paradigm shift in this field, transitioning it from a process reliant on serendipity and laborious experimentation to a data-driven engineering discipline. This review systematically charts the co-evolution of AI methodologies and their transformative applications across the modern biologic drug developmen
papers.summary: This review article examines how artificial intelligence, especially deep learning, is transforming biologic drug discovery from a trial-and-error process into a data-driven engineering field. It systematically covers the evolution of AI methods and their applications in developing protein and nucleic acid therapeutics.

papers.title: Molecular glue degraders: Rational design, specificity engineering, and advanced delivery.
papers.abstract: Molecular glue degraders (MGDs) have emerged as a transformative modality in the field of targeted protein degradation (TPD), enabling the selective elimination of disease-relevant proteins, including those traditionally considered undruggable. Unlike bifunctional proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs), MGDs operate through monovalent architectures that induce protein-protein interactions (PPIs) between E3 ligases and neosubstrates, offering advantages in chemical simplicity, cell permeability, and target scope. However, MGD discovery remains serendipitously, and a translational framework th
papers.summary: This review discusses molecular glue degraders (MGDs), a promising class of drugs that work by inducing interactions between E3 ligases and target proteins to trigger their degradation, offering advantages over traditional PROTACs in simplicity and cell permeability. The authors highlight recent progress in rationally designing MGDs, engineering their specificity, and developing advanced delivery systems to overcome the historically serendipitous nature of their discovery.

papers.title: In Silico Rational Design of Novel 1,3,4-Oxadiazole Thioether Derivatives for the Discovery of Xanthomonas oryzae Inhibitors.
papers.abstract: A series of newly designed 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives demonstrated highly effective inhibitors against Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo) and exhibited significant antiviral and anti-biofilm properties. To further explore the structure-activity relationships of these compounds and develop novel molecules with higher activity, this study systematically analyzed the relationship between the structure and activity of a series of 1,3,4-oxadiazole compounds using computational methods including 3D-QSAR, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations. Based on a dataset containing 39 compo
papers.summary: Researchers used computational methods like 3D-QSAR and molecular docking to design new 1,3,4-oxadiazole thioether derivatives that effectively inhibit the bacterium Xanthomonas oryzae, which causes rice blight. The compounds also showed antiviral and anti-biofilm activity, providing a rational approach for developing novel crop protection agents.

papers.title: De Novo Synthesis in Cancer Immunotherapy: Applications and Prospects.
papers.abstract: De novo protein design enables the creation of proteins with entirely novel structures and biological functions from scratch, offering a promising new paradigm for cancer immunotherapy. Emerging methodologies are integrating principles of rational design, including specificity, modularity, and adaptability, into the development process from the outset. This innovative strategy holds the potential to advance how people understand and intervene against malignancy, through the strategic utilization of immune checkpoint regulation, cytokine engineering, cellular therapy, and signaling pathway remo
papers.summary: This review discusses how de novo protein design—creating proteins with entirely new structures and functions from scratch—can be applied to cancer immunotherapy, including immune checkpoint regulation, cytokine engineering, and cellular therapy. The authors highlight that integrating rational design principles like specificity and modularity from the start could fundamentally change how we understand and treat cancer.

papers.title: Rechargeable N-Halamine Hydrogel Paint Coating Based on Upcycled Sesame Meal Protein for Preventing Cross-Contamination on Food-Contact Surfaces.
papers.abstract: Sesame meal protein isolate (SPI) derived from sesame meal was utilized as an upcycled plant protein source to develop N-halamine structure-based antimicrobial coating system to prevent cross-contamination for fresh food produce. The coating matrix was formulated using SPI, tannic acid (TA), cellulose nanocrystal (CNC), and sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl). The N-halamine structure maintained a stable active chlorine content of approximately 5 µmol/cm2 and enabled repeated recharging to the initial level on the coated model food-contact surface, stainless steel (SS). The stability of the coated sur
papers.summary: This paper describes a new antimicrobial coating made from upcycled sesame meal protein that can be recharged with chlorine to kill bacteria on food-contact surfaces like stainless steel. The coating maintains stable antimicrobial activity and can be recharged multiple times, potentially reducing cross-contamination of fresh produce.

papers.title: Computational and experimental evaluation of Pisolithus arhizus metabolites targeting major efflux pumps of mastitis-associated Staphylococcus aureus.
papers.abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in pathogenic bacteria, particularly Staphylococcus aureus, is an increasing global concern in veterinary medicine. The present study evaluated bioactive metabolites from P. arhizus as potential inhibitors of major S. aureus efflux pumps. The methanolic extracts of P. arhizus were profiled using GC-MS to identify the main constituents, and their antibacterial activity against S. aureus was evaluated using a well diffusion assay. The experimental results showed that the crude methanolic extract exhibited a 20 mm zone of inhibition with a MIC of 30 µg/mL. Furthermo
papers.summary: This study identifies bioactive compounds from the fungus Pisolithus arhizus that can inhibit efflux pumps in mastitis-associated Staphylococcus aureus, offering a potential strategy to combat antimicrobial resistance. The methanolic extract showed strong antibacterial activity with a 20 mm inhibition zone and low MIC value, suggesting these natural metabolites could be developed into alternative treatments for veterinary infections.

papers.title: Multidimensional therapeutic advantages of Smilax glabra (Tufuling)-containing formulae in gout: an integrated Systematic Review and network pharmacology-based prediction.
papers.abstract: Gout, an inflammatory form of arthritis triggered by monosodium urate (MSU) crystal deposition, poses a substantial global health burden with increasing prevalence and younger onset, particularly in China. In traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), dampness-heat accumulation is a predominant pattern associated with gout. Smilax glabra (Tufuling), a medicinal and edible herb with a history of use for detoxification and elimination of dampness, is also known to promote joint mobility. It is widely used for these purposes. This study aimed to systematically evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety o
papers.summary: This study systematically reviews the clinical evidence for Smilax glabra (Tufuling)-containing traditional Chinese medicine formulas in treating gout, finding they may improve symptoms and reduce inflammation markers. The authors also use network pharmacology to predict potential molecular targets and pathways, suggesting the herb works through multiple mechanisms. While promising, the evidence is limited by small sample sizes and variable study quality in the reviewed trials.

papers.title: Integrative serum metabolite prioritization and functional screening identify N-acetyl-L-glutamine as a protective candidate in premature ovarian insufficiency.
papers.abstract: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is a major cause of female infertility and is increasingly associated with systemic metabolic dysregulation. However, whether circulating metabolic alterations contribute causally to POI development or primarily arise as secondary consequences of ovarian failure remains unclear. In this study, bidirectional Mendelian randomization (MR), cell-based screening, and exploratory target-prioritization analyses were integrated to identify POI-related metabolites and functionally relevant candidates.
papers.summary: This study combines Mendelian randomization, cell-based screening, and target prioritization to identify N-acetyl-L-glutamine as a protective metabolite against premature ovarian insufficiency (POI). The findings suggest that this circulating metabolite may play a causal role in POI development, offering a potential therapeutic target for this cause of female infertility.

papers.title: Recent applications of artificial intelligence in cancer radiotherapy and immunotherapy: current status and future directions.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) enhances the precision, personalization, and efficiency of cancer treatment through deep learning and machine learning techniques. This review comprehensively examines the evolution of AI and its expanding applications in cancer radiotherapy, immunotherapy, and drug discovery and repurposing. In radiotherapy, AI enables automated medical image segmentation, thereby facilitating the accurate delineation of tumor targets. Furthermore, AI-driven feedback systems support clinicians in developing individualized treatment plans by offering real-time assessment of treatme
papers.summary: This review article discusses how artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning and machine learning, is improving cancer treatment by enhancing precision and personalization in radiotherapy and immunotherapy. Key applications include automated tumor image segmentation for radiotherapy and AI-driven systems that help clinicians create individualized treatment plans.

papers.title: Identification of key genes potentially associated with bladder cancer development by common plasticizers: an integrated transcriptomics and network toxicology study.
papers.abstract: Bladder cancer (BCa) is a prevalent urological malignancy. The relationship between plasticizers and BCa remains to be elucidated. The present study aimed to investigate the potential relationship between BCa and plasticizers.
papers.summary: This study uses transcriptomics and network toxicology to identify key genes that may link common plasticizers to bladder cancer development. The findings suggest potential molecular mechanisms by which plasticizer exposure could contribute to bladder cancer.

papers.title: Therapeutic potential of Leonotis leonurus and Mentha longifolia in depression: insights from network pharmacology and molecular dynamics stimulation-based study.
papers.abstract: Medicinal plants offer promising multi-target therapeutic agents due to their diverse bioactive compounds. This study explored LC-MS profiling, network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulation to investigate the potential antidepressant-related mechanisms of Leonotis leonurus and Mentha longifolia. A total of 20 and 15 compounds were identified in L. leonurus and M. longifolia, respectively. Target prediction and overlap analysis revealed 16 and 22 depression-associated targets for each plant. These targets were used to construct protein-protein interaction networks a
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify potential antidepressant compounds from two medicinal plants, Leonotis leonurus and Mentha longifolia. By combining LC-MS profiling, network pharmacology, and molecular dynamics simulations, the researchers identified several compounds and their protein targets that may be relevant for treating depression. The work provides a foundation for further experimental validation of these plant-derived compounds as multi-target antidepressant agents.

papers.title: In silico drug discovery and molecular dynamics simulation for targeting neonatal pneumonia and bronchopulmonary dysplasia.
papers.abstract: Neonatal pneumonia and bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) are major causes of morbidity and mortality in preterm infants, driven by excessive inflammation involving the NOD-like receptor family pyrin domain-containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome. This study employed in silico drug discovery, including virtual screening, molecular docking, ADMET profiling, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and MM/PBSA calculations, followed by preliminary in vitro validation to identify novel NLRP3 inhibitors from Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) compounds for these conditions.
papers.summary: This study used computational drug discovery methods to identify potential inhibitors of the NLRP3 inflammasome from Traditional Chinese Medicine compounds, targeting neonatal pneumonia and bronchopulmonary dysplasia. The approach combined virtual screening, molecular docking, and dynamics simulations with preliminary in vitro validation.

papers.title: Anti-amyloid nanobody-Fc fusion protein drives potent amyloid clearance through microglial recruitment.
papers.abstract: Anti-amyloid beta (Aβ) monoclonal antibodies are effective at lowering amyloid in Alzheimer's disease (AD). However, whether Fc-mediated effector function is absolutely required for efficacy is not completely understood. This is important for optimizing therapeutic efficacy and mitigating side effects such as amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA). Antibodies lacking Fc effector function, like single-domain antibodies (nanobodies), offer a unique tool to dissect these mechanisms, as their small size facilitates blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration and allows Fc-mediated functions to be
papers.summary: This study uses anti-amyloid nanobody-Fc fusion proteins to show that Fc-mediated effector function is crucial for amyloid clearance in Alzheimer's disease, as these small proteins can cross the blood-brain barrier and recruit microglia to remove amyloid plaques. The findings help clarify the mechanism of action for antibody-based therapies and may inform strategies to reduce side effects like ARIA.

papers.title: Rational design 2.0: transitioning from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization for RSV.
papers.abstract: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a major cause of severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD) in infants and older adults worldwide. Although vaccines based on the fusion (F) protein have shown progress, their efficacy remains limited by antigenic instability and viral evolution. The metastable transition of the F protein between prefusion (preF) and postF conformations critically determines the exposure of neutralizing epitopes, with most potent antibodies targeting preF-specific sites. In addition, the glycosylated G protein contributes to immune evasion through glycan shielding and CX
papers.summary: This review discusses how computational methods and structural biology are being combined to design better vaccines against respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), focusing on stabilizing the fusion protein in its prefusion conformation to improve immune responses. The authors propose a 'Rational Design 2.0' approach that uses computational prioritization and iterative optimization to overcome challenges like antigenic instability and viral evolution. This strategy aims to enhance vaccine efficacy by targeting the most vulnerable sites on the virus.

papers.title: Structural homology of HHV-6B epitopes as candidates for molecular mimicry triggers of the onset type one diabetes mellitus.
papers.abstract: To study whether HHV-6B can act as a molecular mimic to trigger onset of Type One Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) by assessing structural and binding similarities between HHV-6B derived epitopes and T1DM autoantigen derived epitopes.
papers.summary: This study investigates whether human herpesvirus 6B (HHV-6B) could trigger type 1 diabetes through molecular mimicry, by comparing the structural and binding similarities between viral epitopes and diabetes-related autoantigen epitopes. The findings suggest that HHV-6B epitopes share structural homology with T1DM autoantigens, supporting the hypothesis that viral infection may contribute to autoimmune diabetes onset.
